content_en:
Istiqamah — straightforwardness, uprightness, steadfastness on the path — is a concept so central to Islamic spiritual life that the Quran commands the Prophet ﷺ and his community to be 'istaqim' (steadfast on the right path) in multiple places. The Surah that opens with 'Qul huwa Allahu Ahad' (The Chapter of Sincerity) is followed in the sequence of the Quran by the command: 'So be steadfast as you have been commanded' (Quran 11:112). Al-Nawawi's chapter on istiqamah in Riyadh al-Salihin brings together the essential prophetic guidance on what it means to remain on the straight path consistently, across the length of a lifetime.

The defining hadith of this chapter is the Prophet's ﷺ declaration to Sufyan ibn Abdullah al-Thaqafi, who asked him for a single piece of guidance that would suffice: 'Say: I believe in Allah — and then be steadfast (istaqim).' (Muslim.) This formulation is remarkable for its simplicity and its depth. The entire spiritual life of the Muslim is captured in two movements: the affirmation of faith (iman) and the steadfast maintenance of that faith through consistent righteous action over time. Istiqamah is thus not a single moment of conversion or peak spiritual experience but the daily, unremarkable, and indispensable consistency of the person who has oriented their life toward Allah and refuses to be deflected.

Al-Nawawi also includes the Prophet's ﷺ guidance: 'The most beloved of deeds to Allah are those that are most consistent, even if they are small.' (Al-Bukhari and Muslim.) This hadith directly informs the meaning of istiqamah: it is not the occasional grand spiritual gesture that matters most in the sight of Allah, but the small, consistent deed maintained through rain and shine, through periods of spiritual exaltation and periods of spiritual dryness. A person who prays two rak'ahs of tahajjud (night prayer) every single night for twenty years has done something more beloved to Allah than the person who prays one hundred rak'ahs in a single night of religious enthusiasm and then never prays tahajjud again.

The chapter includes the important qualification that istiqamah does not mean spiritual perfectionism — the demand for a level of consistency that no human being can realistically maintain. The Prophet ﷺ said: 'Do good deeds properly, sincerely, and moderately. Know that your deeds will not make you enter Paradise, and that the most beloved deed to Allah is the most regular and constant, even if it is little.' (Al-Bukhari.) The Shaytan's strategy with the zealous believer is often not to tempt them into obvious sin but to encourage a level of religious enthusiasm that is unsustainable, so that when the inevitable slack comes, discouragement and abandonment follow. Istiqamah — moderate, consistent, sincere — is the remedy to both laxity and unsustainable zeal.

The final verses al-Nawawi cites in this chapter are the Quranic promises attached to istiqamah: 'Indeed, those who say: Our Lord is Allah, and then remain on the right course — the angels will descend upon them, saying: Do not fear, and do not grieve, but receive good tidings of the Paradise which you were promised' (Quran 41:30). Steadfastness, in the Quranic vision, is not merely a moral virtue but a covenant — those who maintain it are accompanied by the angels and promised the greatest of divine gifts.
